quote: I Solar power see, we've got a creek, in our backyard, and I was like, we could do hydro electro in the winter and solar in the summer.
stewart_read: Janice is not just interested in solar — she is actively thinking about energy independence and has already conceptualized a multi-source solution. This is a high-engagement signal: she's a planner, not a passive inquirer. The creek/hydro comment reveals she's thought about this enough to imagine seasonal complementarity. Meg acknowledged it generically ('that makes sense to me') and moved on.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have judged the hydro comment as out of scope and deliberately not engaged to stay on the setter script.
coaching_implication: The strategic read doesn't hold — Meg's on-hold aside at 02:35 ('I know where he told her hydroelectric creek solar panel') confirms she registered the signal and still didn't deploy it. This is a habit gap, not a perception gap. In the 1-on-1, ask Meg: 'You heard the creek comment — what stopped you from anchoring on it?' The answer will tell you whether this is a script-rigidity issue or a confidence issue. The codex's interest_in_solar item explicitly calls for depth and 60+ seconds of expansion — Meg spent under 10 seconds here.

quote: It's a solar panel. It's not about solar panel. I know where he told her hydroelectric creek solar panel. Yeah.
stewart_read: This on-hold aside is the most important moment in the call for coaching purposes. Meg is summarizing the call to herself (or to someone nearby) and explicitly references the creek/hydro comment — she heard it, she processed it, and she still did not use it. This eliminates the charitable read that she missed the signal. The failure to anchor on Janice's energy-independence framing is a deliberate (if unconscious) choice to stay on the script rather than personalize.
coaching_implication: Show Meg this timestamp in the 1-on-1. The question is not 'did you hear it' — she clearly did. The question is 'why didn't you use it?' This is the coaching conversation. The creek/hydro detail was a natural tieback for the text follow-up (codex: text_after_call.required_elements) and a rapport anchor for the closer handoff. Neither happened.

quote: I mean, given that, though, I think you still do get enough sun exposure where it should be fine. I'm not too worried about it, to be honest with you, Chris.
stewart_read: Chris raised a specific, technical concern — north-south roof orientation and seasonal tree shading blocking sun until noon in winter. Meg's response is fabricated confidence: 'I think you still do get enough sun exposure' and 'I'm not too worried about it.' She has no data to support this. The codex's knowledge_gap_handling directive identifies three options: dodge-and-redirect, admit-and-recover-with-transparency, or fabricate. Meg fabricated. The reactive read holds — this is not a strategic deferral, because no setup follows and the concern is never routed to the specialist.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have been attempting to prevent the call from derailing on a technical question she couldn't answer, buying time to get to the credit qualifier.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis has surface plausibility but the execution was wrong — the correct move is transparent deferral to the specialist, not invented reassurance. Suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'That's exactly the kind of thing our specialist will assess with the actual design — they'll look at your roof angle and tree line and tell you exactly what you'd get. That's why the appointment matters.' This routes the concern correctly and creates curiosity rather than closing it with a claim Meg can't back up. If the closer shows up and the shading IS a problem, Meg's reassurance becomes a credibility liability.

quote: Yeah. I share I have it right now with my brother. K. It's my house. It's that's for a in case of death thing.
stewart_read: Janice discloses her brother is on the title as a beneficiary arrangement, not as a co-decision-maker. Meg correctly reads this as non-blocking ('Gotcha. It goes to him.') and moves on. This is the right call — the brother is not a decision-maker and does not need to be on the appointment. The codex's spouse_decision protocol applies to co-decision-makers, not estate beneficiaries. Meg's handling here is clean.
alternative_hypothesis: One could argue Meg should have confirmed the brother's role more explicitly before dismissing the title question.
coaching_implication: No coaching needed on this specific moment — Meg read the title situation correctly. Worth noting as a positive example of protocol judgment: she distinguished between 'on the title' and 'decision-maker' without being prompted. The manager can use this as a contrast point when coaching the spouse-protocol on other calls.

quote: Around a 150, I guess. Let's put that here. I mean, I've seen it low in the last year down. Lowest is 70 something. Last year, it was 200 and something. So
stewart_read: Chris provides a bill range with real texture — $70 low, $200+ high, $150 average. This is a rich anchor: the variance itself is a pain point (unpredictability on top of cost). Meg's response is 'Yeah. Let's definitely see how we can help with that and, you know, avoid those super high bills. Right?' — she captures the number but does not flip it. The codex's bill_anchoring section explicitly names this failure pattern: 'ask + acknowledge + move on' instead of 'ask + acknowledge + flip.'
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have been holding the flip for after the design, letting the closer deploy it with actual numbers.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis doesn't hold — the setter's job is to create emotional alignment-against-the-utility before the closer arrives, not to leave it for the closer. The $200+ high bill is the flip opportunity: the unpredictability of that range is the pain, and solar replaces the variable with a fixed cost. Suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'Two hundred dollars — and you never know when it's coming. That's the part solar fixes: you know exactly what you're paying every month.' No fabricated numbers needed; Chris gave the $200 figure himself.

quote: Now I I won't be able to get that scheduled until I receive a photo of the bill. Is Janice working on that?
stewart_read: Meg uses the bill requirement as a soft conditional close — she names the time slot (tomorrow 10AM) before the bill arrives, then ties confirmation to receipt of the photo. This is a reasonable sequencing move that keeps Chris engaged while Janice works on the photo. The bill arrives at 08:50 and Meg confirms the appointment. However, the button-up is incomplete: no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, no explicit one-hour block stated, no 'not driving / at computer' anchor per the codex's required button-up elements.
coaching_implication: The conditional structure worked — the bill came through and the appointment was set. The gap is in the button-up completeness. The manager should walk Meg through the codex's button_up required elements and ask her to identify which ones she hit. Missing the 'is that fair' handshake and the computer/not-driving anchor are the two highest-impact omissions for show rate. This is a checklist discipline issue, not a judgment issue.

quote: Well, I appreciate you guys in in getting me everything. We'll plan on speaking tomorrow at 10AM.
stewart_read: Meg closes the call without mentioning the follow-up text. The codex requires a personalized text with a tieback to something specific the customer said. Janice's creek/hydro comment was the natural tieback — it was memorable, specific, and would have differentiated the text from a generic confirmation. Meg had the material (she referenced it on hold at 02:35) and did not use it.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may send the text after the call ends — the transcript doesn't capture post-call activity.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis is possible but the codex requires the rep to PROMISE the text on the call ('Inform them you'll send a text with appointment details after the call'). Meg did not make that promise. In the 1-on-1, confirm whether the text was sent and whether it included a tieback. If it was generic or not sent, this is the third place the creek/hydro signal was available and unused — a pattern worth naming explicitly to Meg.
